steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: Sweanor (radio-collar tracking, New Mexico) found 2.00 cougars per 100 sq km; Choate (helicopter surveying, Utah) found 10.24 per 100 sq km — a gap of 8.24. The question asks which explanation would make that reported gap artificially large, meaning the true gap is actually smaller.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — radio-collar tracking is impractical outside of New Mexico: Addresses method logistics rather than systematic measurement bias that would distort the density count. ✗
  • Choice B — radio-collar tracking underestimates the density of cougars: If Sweanor's method undercounts, his 2.00 figure is artificially low — the true New Mexico density is higher, so the actual gap between the two regions is smaller than the reported 8.24. ✓
  • Choice C — helicopter surveying underestimates the density of cougars: Would cause the gap to be understated, not artificially inflated. ✗
  • Choice D — helicopter surveying is more common in Utah than the use of radio-collar tracking is: Addresses prevalence of method use rather than whether the method produces biased density estimates. ✗
That confirms B is correct.
